title_en: Introduction: Islamic Fiqh and Its Sources
content_en:
Al-Fiqh al-Islami wa-Adillatuhu by Dr. Wahbah al-Zuhayli stands as the most comprehensive contemporary encyclopedia of Islamic jurisprudence in the Arabic language. Written over many years and published in its expanded edition in eight volumes, the work covers virtually every chapter of classical fiqh across all four recognized Sunni schools of law, presenting each ruling alongside its evidence from the Quran, the Sunnah, scholarly consensus, and analogical reasoning. No comparable modern work matches its breadth, depth, and systematic organization.

The introductory section establishes the intellectual framework of the entire encyclopedia. Dr. al-Zuhayli defines fiqh in both its original broad sense — comprehensive understanding of the religion — and its technical jurisprudential sense: knowledge of the practical rulings of the Shari'ah derived from their detailed proofs. He traces the historical development of fiqh from the era of the Prophet, peace be upon him, through the Companions and Followers, the formation of the schools, the era of imitation (taqlid), and the contemporary period of revival and reform.

The sources of Islamic law are treated with scholarly rigor. The Quran as the primary source, the Sunnah as the second source that explains and complements the Quran, ijma' (scholarly consensus) and its various categories, and qiyas (analogical reasoning) with its conditions and types are all examined. Dr. al-Zuhayli also addresses secondary sources recognized by some or all schools: istihsan (juristic preference), maslaha mursalah (public interest), urf (custom), sadd al-dhara'i (blocking the means to harm), and others, explaining each school's acceptance or rejection of these secondary sources.

The introduction concludes with a discussion of the methodology of comparative fiqh — the analytical approach that Dr. al-Zuhayli employs throughout the encyclopedia. Rather than advocating blind following of one school, he presents the evidence for each school's position on contested questions, allowing readers to appreciate the richness of jurisprudential diversity while understanding that differences among the schools represent legitimate scholarly ijtihad rather than contradiction or error.
